source_title: Common Flowering Plants (Forbs) of South Dakota

plants.identification: A small (less than 1 ft. tall) native perennial. Leaves are mostly basal and are dissected into many narrow, linear divisions, and are densely silky when young. It has tulip-like flowers that vary in color from white to violet.
plants.habitat: Found in prairies, on grassy hillsides, and in open wooded areas across South Dakota. Prefers part shade or sun and dry sandy soil.
plants.rangeland_considerations: State Flower of South Dakota. Important resource for the first pollinators of spring. Usually avoided by livestock. If grazed in high quantity, the sap can cause blistering.

plants.identification: A short, mat-forming native that has many branching stems from the crown. Oblong leaves are opposite and fringed with marginal hairs. Has white, pink, or light purple flowers.
plants.habitat: Primarily found in western South Dakota; prefers dry, open grasslands and rocky slopes. Fairly drought resistant.
plants.rangeland_considerations: Generally not considered a forage species; utilized by wildlife.

plants.identification: A native vine-like (slender and trailing) perennial with blue to purple (rarely white) flowers. Leaves are divided into 8-18 linear/oval leaflets with tendrils at the ends. Has bean-like seed pods.
plants.habitat: Occurs in most South Dakota plant communities including grasslands, foothills, and mountains. Tolerates a wide range of soil moisture.
plants.rangeland_considerations: A legume that can fix nitrogen. High palatability for livestock and excellent forage. Decreases with heavy grazing.

plants.identification: Grows up to 1.5 ft tall. First year produces a basal rosette. Second year produces multiple stems with alternate lanceolate leaves and sweet-smelling yellow four-petaled flowers.
plants.habitat: Primarily found in upland prairies. Can tolerate a range of soil and moisture conditions.
plants.rangeland_considerations: Offers pollinators abundant resources early in the season. Wildlife will graze it; may not offer much for livestock due to small size in year one.

plants.identification: Has a basal rosette and 12-30 inch tall, smooth, hollow stem. Yellow flower head turns into a large dandelion-style 'puffball'. Leaves and stems contain bitter, milky juice.
plants.habitat: Occurs widely around South Dakota, especially in disturbed areas.
plants.rangeland_considerations: Frequently grazed by livestock and is a food source for sharp-tailed grouse.

plants.identification: Grows 1-3 feet tall with showy flowers containing 6-18, 3-lobed yellow ray petals surrounding a brownish or reddish disc. Stems and leaves are covered in dense hairs.
plants.habitat: Found in sunny, dry prairies and grassy openings in mountains. Drought tolerant.
plants.rangeland_considerations: Sheep may graze it, but usually ignored by other livestock. Attracts many pollinators; upland game birds eat the seeds.

plants.identification: Gray-green leaves are alternate and deeply lobed with tiny star-shaped silvery hairs. Flowers are orange or brick red.
plants.habitat: Common in the west. One of the most drought-tolerant rangeland flowers in South Dakota.
plants.rangeland_considerations: Occasionally grazed by livestock; increases with heavy grazing. Used by wildlife, insects, and has traditional ethnobotanical uses.

plants.identification: Grows 1-2 feet tall. Alternate leaves with 3-5 palmately arranged leaflets covered in silky white hairs, giving it a silvery appearance. Produces small blue flowers.
plants.habitat: Common in open prairies and evident on hillsides and slopes.
plants.rangeland_considerations: A legume that can fix nitrogen. Utilized more by wildlife than livestock.

plants.identification: Grows 2-3 feet tall. Branches terminate in a dense spike of purple flowers with orange anthers.
plants.habitat: Common in many South Dakota rangelands; tolerates various soil and moisture conditions.
plants.rangeland_considerations: A nitrogen-fixing legume. Excellent forage that can decrease with heavy grazing. Used by numerous native pollinators.

plants.identification: First year produces a fuzzy basal rosette. Second year grows a single tall (up to 8 ft) flowering stem with yellow flowers.
plants.habitat: Found in disturbed areas, more common in western South Dakota. Listed as a noxious weed in many counties.
plants.rangeland_considerations: Not useful as forage for livestock, though safe and palatable. Can become abundant in grazing lands.

plants.identification: Toxic and invasive. Grows 1-3 feet high in thick clumps. Has yellow-green bracts and a milky sap that can cause blistering.
plants.habitat: Habitat generalist found in moist or dry pastures and mountains.
plants.rangeland_considerations: Usually avoided by livestock. Toxic sap. Sheep and goats can be trained to eat it.

plants.identification: Feathery or fern-like alternate leaves. Flattened dome inflorescence covered with white flowers.
plants.habitat: Widespread and abundant in disturbed areas. Drought tolerant.
plants.rangeland_considerations: Not palatable to most livestock and mildly toxic to horses. May give cattle milk a 'disagreeable' flavor.

plants.identification: Grows 12-36 inches tall. Distinct flowers with bright yellow rays and a columnar disk.
plants.habitat: Likes rich loamy soil and full sun. Establishes easily from seed.
plants.rangeland_considerations: Palatable to livestock and wildlife. Attracts many pollinators; birds eat the seeds.

plants.identification: Square stems (Mint family) and minty-smelling foliage. Grows 2-4 feet tall with tubular purple to pink flowers.
plants.habitat: Occurs in moist to average soils. Often spreads in clumpy patches.
plants.rangeland_considerations: Attracts butterflies and hummingbirds. Not grazed much by livestock/wildlife; a 'decreaser' with heavy grazing.

plants.identification: Tall (up to 5 ft) with one big stem topped with loose panicles of numerous small white flowers.
plants.habitat: Widespread in disturbed areas; tolerates many soil/moisture conditions.
plants.rangeland_considerations: Considered mildly toxic and usually avoided by livestock due to irritating chemicals.

plants.identification: Grows 1-3 feet tall. Yellow flowers have sticky, resinous hook-shaped bracts on the back.
plants.habitat: Found in prairies and disturbed landscapes like road edges or overgrazed rangeland.
plants.rangeland_considerations: Not palatable to livestock. Can accumulate selenium in high-selenium soils and become toxic.

plants.identification: Aromatic, silvery-gray plant growing up to 3 feet tall. Inconspicuous small yellow flowers.
plants.habitat: Common across South Dakota, including upland prairies and roadsides in the Black Hills.
plants.rangeland_considerations: Important forage for elk and pronghorn. Generally not palatable to domestic livestock. Substantial ethnobotanical use.
